        f.'Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12)' AS frpm_percent,
        PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY f.'Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12)' DESC) AS frpm_rank
    FROM frpm f
    JOIN schools s ON f.CDSCode = s.CDSCode
    WHERE s.StatusType = 'Active'
),
HighFRPMSchools AS (
    SELECT 
        CDSCode,
        frpm_percent
    FROM FRPMAverages
    WHERE frpm_rank <= 0.2
)
SELECT 
    s.School,
    s.District,
    f.'Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12)'
FROM HighFRPMSchools hfr
JOIN frpm f ON hfr.CDSCode = f.CDSCode
JOIN schools s ON f.CDSCode = s.CDSCode
ORDER BY hfr.frpm_percent DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'notably high', I mean we should focus on the top 20% of schools with the highest FRPM eligibility rates relative to all active schools in the dataset.

question: Schools with high average reading scores.
sql: SELECT * FROM satscores WHERE AvgScrRead > 500;
hidden_knowledge: By high average reading scores, I mean schools where students scored above 500 on average in reading.

question: Show schools with high average math SAT scores.
sql: WITH MathScoreRank AS (SELECT s.cds, s.AvgScrMath, ROW_NUMBER() OVER (ORDER BY s.AvgScrMath DESC) AS rn FROM satscores s WHERE s.rtype = 'S' AND s.AvgScrMath IS NOT NULL) SELECT sc.School, sc.City, ms.AvgScrMath FROM MathScoreRank ms JOIN schools sc ON ms.cds = sc.CDSCode WHERE ms.rn <= (SELECT COUNT(*) FROM satscores WHERE rtype = 'S' AND AvgScrMath IS NOT NULL) * 0.1;
hidden_knowledge: By high average math SAT scores, I mean the top 10% of schools ranked by their average math scores.

question: Schools with high SAT scores.
sql:
WITH CompositeScores AS (
    SELECT 
        s.cds,
        (s.AvgScrRead + s.AvgScrMath + s.AvgScrWrite) AS TotalAvg,
        PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY (s.AvgScrRead + s.AvgScrMath + s.AvgScrWrite) DESC) AS PctRank
    FROM satscores s
    WHERE s.rtype = 'S' 
      AND s.NumTstTakr > 50
),
RankedSchools AS (
    SELECT 
        cs.cds,
        cs.TotalAvg,
        cs.PctRank,
        sc.School
    FROM CompositeScores cs
    JOIN schools sc ON cs.cds = sc.CDSCode
)
SELECT rs.School, rs.TotalAvg
FROM RankedSchools rs
WHERE rs.PctRank < 0.1
ORDER BY rs.TotalAvg DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By high SAT scores, I mean schools in the top 10% based on total average composite score (sum of reading, math, and writing).

question: Retrieve the details of users who have received a significant number of badges, focusing on those with the most prestigious distinctions.
sql:
WITH UserBadgeTypes AS ( 
    SELECT 
        u.Id, 
        u.DisplayName, 
        COUNT(DISTINCT b.Name) AS TotalBadges, 
        MAX(CASE WHEN b.Name = 'Teacher' THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS HasTeacherBadge, 
        SUM(CASE WHEN b.Name IN ('Teacher', 'Moderator') THEN 1 ELSE 0 END) AS PrestigeBadges 
    FROM 
        users u 
    LEFT JOIN 
        badges b ON u.Id = b.UserId 
    GROUP BY 
        u.Id, u.DisplayName 
), RankedUsers AS (
    SELECT 
        Id, DisplayName, TotalBadges, PrestigeBadges,
        HasTeacherBadge,
        PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY CASE WHEN HasTeacherBadge = 1 THEN PrestigeBadges ELSE TotalBadges END DESC) AS PercentRank
    FROM
        UserBadgeTypes
)
SELECT
    Id, DisplayName, TotalBadges, PrestigeBadges
FROM
    RankedUsers
WHERE
    PercentRank < 0.1;
hidden_knowledge: By most prestigious distinctions, I mean focusing on the top 10% of users either by their count of high-prestige badges (Teacher or Moderator) or by their total badge count if they don't have a Teacher badge.

question: Hey buddy! Can you find all the posts with many answers? I'm interested in the most discussed topics.
sql: SELECT Id, Title, AnswerCount FROM posts WHERE AnswerCount > (SELECT AVG(AnswerCount) FROM posts) ORDER BY AnswerCount DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many answers,' I mean posts with answers above the average answer count.

question: Hey, can you show me the top users based on their reputation? I'd love to see who's really popular around here.
sql: WITH UserReputation AS ( SELECT Id, DisplayName, Reputation, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY Reputation DESC) AS reputation_rank FROM users ) SELECT ur.DisplayName, ur.Reputation FROM UserReputation ur WHERE ur.reputation_rank <= 10;
hidden_knowledge: By 'popular', I mean users with the highest reputation scores.

question: Hey, can you show me the top users based on their reputation? I'd love to see who's really popular around here.
sql: WITH UserActivity AS ( SELECT u.Id, u.DisplayName, COUNT(v.Id) AS vote_count, RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(v.Id) DESC) AS activity_rank FROM users u LEFT JOIN votes v ON u.Id = v.UserId GROUP BY u.Id, u.DisplayName ) SELECT ua.DisplayName, ua.vote_count FROM UserActivity ua WHERE ua.activity_rank <= 10;
hidden_knowledge: By 'popular', I mean users who have received the most votes, indicating higher activity.

question: List popular tags.
sql: SELECT Id, TagName, Count FROM tags WHERE Count > 100 ORDER BY Count DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By popular tags, I mean those used more than 100 times.

question: Who are the high-reputation users on this platform?
sql: SELECT DisplayName FROM users WHERE Reputation > 1000 ORDER BY Reputation DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'high-reputation', I mean users with more than 1,000 reputation points.

question: I'm analyzing user engagement on our platform and want to identify which posts have received positive feedback from the community. Could you show me the posts with many upvotes so I can study their content and popularity trends?
sql: SELECT Title, Score FROM posts WHERE Score > 50 ORDER BY Score DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many upvotes', I mean posts with a score higher than 50.

question: Show popular tags.
sql: SELECT TagName, Count FROM tags WHERE Count > 100 ORDER BY Count DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By popular tags, I mean those used more than 100 times.

question: List the products purchased during frequent transactions.
sql: SELECT p.ProductID, p.Description FROM products p INNER JOIN transactions_1k t ON p.ProductID = t.ProductID WHERE t.CustomerID IN (SELECT CustomerID FROM transactions_1k GROUP BY CustomerID HAVING COUNT(TransactionID) > 10);
hidden_knowledge: By frequent transactions, I mean customers who have conducted a large number of transactions.

question: Hey, can you show me the gas stations that have a lot of transactions? I'm trying to figure out which ones are super busy.
sql: SELECT gs.GasStationID, gs.ChainID, gs.Country, gs.Segment, COUNT(t.TransactionID) as transaction_count FROM gasstations gs JOIN transactions_1k t ON gs.GasStationID = t.GasStationID GROUP BY gs.GasStationID, gs.ChainID, gs.Country, gs.Segment HAVING COUNT(t.TransactionID) > (SELECT AVG(transaction_count) FROM (SELECT COUNT(TransactionID) as transaction_count FROM transactions_1k GROUP BY GasStationID) subq)
hidden_knowledge: By 'a lot of transactions,' I mean more than the average number of transactions per gas station.

question: Hey, can you show me the gas stations that have a lot of transactions? I'm trying to figure out which ones are super busy.
sql: WITH StationStats AS (SELECT GasStationID, COUNT(TransactionID) as transaction_count, PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(TransactionID) DESC) as percentile FROM transactions_1k GROUP BY GasStationID), TopStations AS (SELECT GasStationID FROM StationStats WHERE percentile < 0.1) SELECT gs.GasStationID, gs.ChainID, gs.Country, gs.Segment FROM gasstations gs JOIN TopStations ts ON gs.GasStationID = ts.GasStationID
hidden_knowledge: By 'a lot of transactions,' I mean in the top 10% of gas stations by transaction volume.

question: Display customers with significant consumption variations between consecutive months
sql:
WITH MonthlyConsumption AS (
    SELECT
        CustomerID,
        Date,
        Consumption,
        LAG(Consumption) OVER (PARTITION BY CustomerID ORDER BY CAST(Date AS INTEGER)) AS PreviousMonthConsumption
    FROM yearmonth
),
ConsumptionVariations AS (
    SELECT
        CustomerID,
        Date,
        Consumption,
        PreviousMonthConsumption,
        (Consumption - PreviousMonthConsumption) AS Variation,
        ABS(Consumption - PreviousMonthConsumption) AS AbsoluteVariation
    FROM MonthlyConsumption
    WHERE PreviousMonthConsumption IS NOT NULL
),
SignificantVariations AS (
    SELECT
        cv.*
    FROM ConsumptionVariations cv
    WHERE cv.AbsoluteVariation > (
        SELECT AVG(ABS(c.AbsoluteVariation)) * 2
        FROM ConsumptionVariations c
    )
)
SELECT
    s.CustomerID,
    s.Date,
    s.Consumption,
    s.PreviousMonthConsumption,
    s.Variation,
    s.AbsoluteVariation
FROM SignificantVariations s
ORDER BY s.AbsoluteVariation DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By significant consumption variations, I mean customers whose absolute consumption difference between consecutive months is greater than twice the average variation across all customers.

question: Display customers with significant consumption variations between consecutive months
sql:
WITH MonthlyConsumption AS (
    SELECT
        CustomerID,
        Date,
        Consumption,
        LAG(Consumption) OVER (PARTITION BY CustomerID ORDER BY CAST(Date AS INTEGER)) AS PreviousMonthConsumption
    FROM yearmonth
),
ConsumptionVariations AS (
    SELECT
        CustomerID,
        Date,
        Consumption,
        PreviousMonthConsumption,
        (Consumption - PreviousMonthConsumption) / NULLIF(PreviousMonthConsumption, 0) * 100 AS PercentageVariation
    FROM MonthlyConsumption
    WHERE PreviousMonthConsumption IS NOT NULL
),
SignificantVariations AS (
    SELECT
        cv.*
    FROM ConsumptionVariations cv
    WHERE cv.PercentageVariation > 50 OR cv.PercentageVariation < -50 
)
SELECT
    s.CustomerID,
    s.Date,
    s.Consumption,
    s.PreviousMonthConsumption,
    s.PercentageVariation
FROM SignificantVariations s
ORDER BY ABS(s.PercentageVariation) DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By significant consumption variations, I mean customers whose consumption changes by more than 50% (either increase or decrease) between consecutive months.

question: Find patients with persistently high LDH levels over multiple examinations.
sql: SELECT DISTINCT p.ID, p.SEX, p.Birthday FROM Patient p INNER JOIN Laboratory l ON p.ID = l.ID WHERE l.LDH > 400 GROUP BY p.ID HAVING COUNT(*) >= 3;
hidden_knowledge: By persistently high LDH levels over multiple examinations, I mean LDH levels greater than 400 in at least three different examinations.

question: Hi! Can you show me the popular symptoms among patients? Just trying to see what's common lately.
sql: SELECT Symptoms, COUNT(*) AS symptom_count FROM Examination WHERE Symptoms IS NOT NULL GROUP BY Symptoms HAVING COUNT(*) > 5 ORDER BY symptom_count DESC LIMIT 5;
hidden_knowledge: 'Popular' means symptoms that appear in more than 5 examination records.

question: Which patients had high platelet counts in their laboratory results?
sql: SELECT ID FROM Laboratory WHERE PLT > 300;
hidden_knowledge: By high platelet counts, I mean values greater than 300.

question: Popular diagnoses in examinations
sql: SELECT Diagnosis, COUNT(*) AS frequency FROM Examination GROUP BY Diagnosis HAVING COUNT(*) > 5 ORDER BY frequency DESC LIMIT 5;
hidden_knowledge: By popular diagnoses, I mean those occurring more than 5 times.

question: Retrieve details of events with significant attendance figures.
sql: SELECT e.event_id, e.event_name, e.event_date, COUNT(a.link_to_member) AS attendance_count FROM event e LEFT JOIN attendance a ON e.event_id = a.link_to_event GROUP BY e.event_id, e.event_name, e.event_date HAVING COUNT(a.link_to_member) > (SELECT AVG(attendance_count) * 1.5 FROM (SELECT COUNT(link_to_member) AS attendance_count FROM attendance GROUP BY link_to_event) AS avg_attendance) ORDER BY attendance_count DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By significant attendance figures, I mean events with attendance counts that are 50% above the average attendance across all events.

question: Find members who have attended many events
sql: SELECT m.member_id, m.first_name, m.last_name, COUNT(a.link_to_event) AS events_attended FROM member m INNER JOIN attendance a ON m.member_id = a.link_to_member GROUP BY m.member_id, m.first_name, m.last_name HAVING COUNT(a.link_to_event) > 10 ORDER BY events_attended DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By many events, I mean members who have attended more than 10 events.

question: Display events with high attendance
sql: WITH event_attendance AS ( SELECT e.event_id, e.event_name, COUNT(a.link_to_member) AS attendance_count FROM event e JOIN attendance a ON e.event_id = a.link_to_event GROUP BY e.event_id, e.event_name ) SELECT event_id, event_name, attendance_count FROM event_attendance WHERE attendance_count = (SELECT MAX(attendance_count) FROM event_attendance);
hidden_knowledge: By high attendance, I mean the events with the maximum number of attendees.

question: I'm planning next year's event schedule and need to identify popular meeting locations that have been well-received by members in the past. Could you list these locations along with the types of events that were held there?
sql: WITH location_popularity AS ( SELECT location, COUNT(*) AS event_count FROM event WHERE location IS NOT NULL AND status = 'Closed' GROUP BY location ) SELECT DISTINCT e.location, e.type FROM event e JOIN location_popularity lp ON e.location = lp.location WHERE lp.event_count > (SELECT AVG(event_count) * 1.2 FROM location_popularity) ORDER BY lp.event_count DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'popular meeting locations,' I mean locations that have hosted more than 1.2 times the average number of events.

question: Events with significant income
sql: SELECT i.source, SUM(i.amount) AS total_income FROM income i GROUP BY i.source HAVING SUM(i.amount) > 200;
hidden_knowledge: By significant income, I mean sources that generated over $200 in total.

question: Members from well-funded majors
sql: WITH major_income AS (SELECT maj.major_id, SUM(i.amount) AS total_income FROM major maj LEFT JOIN member m ON maj.major_id = m.link_to_major LEFT JOIN income i ON m.member_id = i.link_to_member GROUP BY maj.major_id), avg_income AS (SELECT AVG(total_income) AS avg_income FROM major_income WHERE total_income IS NOT NULL) SELECT m.first_name, m.last_name, maj.department FROM member m INNER JOIN major maj ON m.link_to_major = maj.major_id INNER JOIN major_income mi ON maj.major_id = mi.major_id INNER JOIN avg_income ai ON mi.total_income > ai.avg_income;
hidden_knowledge: By well-funded majors, I mean departments whose members have generated above-average income.

question: Display drivers with many pit stops during races
sql: WITH FrequentPitStoppers AS ( SELECT driverId, COUNT(*) AS pitStopCount FROM pitStops GROUP BY driverId HAVING COUNT(*) > 20 ) SELECT d.forename, d.surname, d.nationality, fps.pitStopCount FROM drivers d JOIN FrequentPitStoppers fps ON d.driverId = fps.driverId ORDER BY fps.pitStopCount DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By many pit stops, I mean drivers who have made more than twenty pit stops across all races.

question: Which circuits are located in popular countries for Formula 1?
sql: SELECT c.name, c.country, COUNT(r.raceId) AS race_count FROM circuits c LEFT JOIN races r ON c.circuitId = r.circuitId GROUP BY c.name, c.country HAVING COUNT(r.raceId) > 30 ORDER BY race_count DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'popular,' I mean countries that have hosted more than 30 races.

question: Top performing drivers
sql: SELECT d.forename, d.surname, SUM(ds.points) AS total_points FROM drivers d INNER JOIN driverStandings ds ON d.driverId = ds.driverId GROUP BY d.driverId ORDER BY total_points DESC LIMIT 5;
hidden_knowledge: By 'top performing,' I mean drivers with the highest cumulative points across all races.

question: Top performing drivers
sql: SELECT d.forename, d.surname, AVG(ds.points) AS average_points FROM drivers d INNER JOIN driverStandings ds ON d.driverId = ds.driverId GROUP BY d.driverId HAVING AVG(ds.points) > (SELECT AVG(points) FROM driverStandings) ORDER BY average_points DESC LIMIT 5;
hidden_knowledge: By 'top performing,' I mean drivers who consistently score above the average points per race.

question: Popular districts by account frequency.
sql: SELECT district_id, COUNT(*) as account_count FROM account WHERE frequency = 'POPLATEK MESICNE' GROUP BY district_id ORDER BY account_count DESC LIMIT 5;
hidden_knowledge: By popular districts, I mean the top five districts by account frequency.

question: Find molecules with an unusual bonding pattern.
sql: SELECT DISTINCT b1.molecule_id, b1.bond_type AS bond_type1, b2.bond_type AS bond_type2 FROM bond b1 JOIN bond b2 ON b1.molecule_id = b2.molecule_id AND b1.bond_type != b2.bond_type WHERE (b1.bond_type IN ('=', '#') OR b2.bond_type IN ('=', '#')) AND (b1.bond_type IN ('-', '=') OR b2.bond_type IN ('-', '='));
hidden_knowledge: By 'unusual bonding pattern', I mean molecules with any combination of different bond types present.

question: Howdy! Can you show me the small molecules in the database? I need to analyze their properties.
sql: SELECT m.molecule_id, m.label, COUNT(a.atom_id) AS atom_count FROM molecule m INNER JOIN atom a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id GROUP BY m.molecule_id, m.label HAVING COUNT(a.atom_id) < 5;
hidden_knowledge: By 'small molecules', I mean those with fewer than 5 atoms.

question: Hey there! I need to see some complex molecules with a lot of atoms. Can you help me find those?
sql: WITH atom_counts AS (SELECT molecule_id, COUNT(atom_id) AS atom_count FROM atom GROUP BY molecule_id), bond_counts AS (SELECT molecule_id, COUNT(bond_id) AS bond_count FROM bond GROUP BY molecule_id) SELECT m.molecule_id, m.label, ac.atom_count FROM molecule m JOIN atom_counts ac ON m.molecule_id = ac.molecule_id JOIN bond_counts bc ON m.molecule_id = bc.molecule_id WHERE ac.atom_count > 10 AND bc.bond_count > 15;
hidden_knowledge: By 'a lot' I mean more than 10 atoms and more than 15 bonds in the molecule.

question: Find molecules with many atoms
sql: SELECT m.molecule_id, m.label FROM molecule m JOIN atom a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id GROUP BY m.molecule_id, m.label HAVING COUNT(a.atom_id) > 10;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many atoms', I mean molecules that have more than 10 atoms.

question: Which molecules contain many atoms?
sql: SELECT molecule_id, COUNT(*) AS atom_count FROM atom GROUP BY molecule_id HAVING COUNT(*) > 5;
hidden_knowledge: By many, I mean molecules with more than 5 atoms.

question: I'm conducting research on molecular structures and need to identify molecules that contain a significant number of chlorine atoms. This will help me understand the prevalence of chlorine in various chemical compounds. The results will be used to assess the environmental impact of these molecules.
sql:
WITH ChlorineCount AS (
    SELECT
        a.molecule_id,
        COUNT(*) AS chlorine_count
    FROM
        atom a
    WHERE
        a.element = 'cl'
    GROUP BY
        a.molecule_id
)
SELECT
    m.molecule_id,
    m.label,
    cc.chlorine_count
FROM
    molecule m
JOIN
    ChlorineCount cc ON m.molecule_id = cc.molecule_id
WHERE
    cc.chlorine_count > 3;
hidden_knowledge: By a significant number of chlorine atoms, I mean molecules that have more than three chlorine atoms.

question: Large molecular structures.
sql: WITH molecular_size AS ( SELECT a.molecule_id, COUNT(DISTINCT a.atom_id) AS size FROM atom a GROUP BY a.molecule_id ) SELECT DISTINCT m.molecule_id, m.label FROM molecule m JOIN molecular_size ms ON m.molecule_id = ms.molecule_id WHERE ms.size > 30;
hidden_knowledge: By large molecular structures, I mean those with more than 30 atoms.

question: Retrieve the attributes of players who possess high overall ratings.
sql: SELECT p.player_name, pa.overall_rating, pa.potential FROM Player p INNER JOIN Player_Attributes pa ON p.player_api_id = pa.player_api_id WHERE pa.overall_rating >= 85;
hidden_knowledge: By high overall ratings, I mean ratings of 85 or above.

question: Hey, can you show me the top-performing players in terms of overall rating? I'm really curious about those with high potential too.
sql: WITH avg_stats AS (SELECT AVG(overall_rating) as avg_rating, AVG(potential) as avg_potential FROM Player_Attributes) SELECT p.player_name, pa.overall_rating, pa.potential FROM Player p JOIN Player_Attributes pa ON p.player_api_id = pa.player_api_id CROSS JOIN avg_stats WHERE pa.overall_rating > avg_stats.avg_rating AND pa.potential > avg_stats.avg_potential ORDER BY pa.overall_rating DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By top-performing, I mean players who have an overall rating above the average, and by high potential, I mean those with potential above the average.

question: List leagues with many competitive matches
sql: SELECT l.name FROM League l INNER JOIN Match m ON l.id = m.league_id GROUP BY l.name HAVING AVG(m.home_team_goal + m.away_team_goal) > 2.5;
hidden_knowledge: By many competitive matches, I mean leagues where the average total goals per match is greater than 2.5.

question: Players with high overall ratings.
sql: WITH PlayerAvgRating AS (SELECT player_api_id, AVG(overall_rating) AS avg_rating FROM Player_Attributes GROUP BY player_api_id) SELECT p.player_name, par.avg_rating FROM Player p JOIN PlayerAvgRating par ON p.player_api_id = par.player_api_id WHERE par.avg_rating > 75;
hidden_knowledge: By high overall ratings, I mean players whose average overall rating across all their records is above 75.

question: Hey buddy! Can you show me the heavy hitters in the superhero world? You know, the ones who pack a punch.
sql: WITH heavy_hitters AS ( SELECT hero_id, SUM(attribute_value) as total_power FROM hero_attribute GROUP BY hero_id HAVING SUM(attribute_value) > 300 ) SELECT s.superhero_name, hh.total_power FROM superhero s JOIN heavy_hitters hh ON s.id = hh.hero_id ORDER BY hh.total_power DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By heavy hitters, I mean those with combined attribute values over 300.

question: Hey buddy! Can you show me the heavy hitters in the superhero world? You know, the ones who pack a punch.
sql: WITH heavy_hitters AS ( SELECT hero_id, SUM(attribute_value) as total_power FROM hero_attribute GROUP BY hero_id HAVING SUM(attribute_value) > 250 ) SELECT s.superhero_name, hh.total_power FROM superhero s JOIN heavy_hitters hh ON s.id = hh.hero_id WHERE EXISTS ( SELECT 1 FROM hero_power hp JOIN superpower sp ON hp.power_id = sp.id WHERE hp.hero_id = hh.hero_id AND sp.power_name LIKE '%Strength%' ) ORDER BY hh.total_power DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By heavy hitters, I mean those with combined attribute values over 250 who also have strength-related powers.

question: Display superheroes with a unique combination of powers and attributes
sql: WITH PowerCount AS ( SELECT hero_power.hero_id, COUNT(*) AS power_count FROM hero_power GROUP BY hero_power.hero_id ), AttributeCount AS ( SELECT hero_attribute.hero_id, COUNT(*) AS attribute_count FROM hero_attribute GROUP BY hero_attribute.hero_id ) SELECT s.superhero_name, pc.power_count + ac.attribute_count AS total_features FROM superhero s JOIN PowerCount pc ON s.id = pc.hero_id JOIN AttributeCount ac ON s.id = ac.hero_id WHERE pc.power_count >= 5 AND ac.attribute_count >= 3;
hidden_knowledge: By a unique combination of powers and attributes, I mean superheroes who have at least five powers and three attributes

question: Display superheroes with significant power attributes
sql:
WITH PowerfulHeroes AS (
    SELECT s.id, s.superhero_name, s.full_name, ha.attribute_value
    FROM superhero s
    JOIN hero_attribute ha ON s.id = ha.hero_id
    WHERE ha.attribute_id = 1 -- Intelligence
    AND ha.attribute_value > 90
    UNION
    SELECT s.id, s.superhero_name, s.full_name, ha.attribute_value
    FROM superhero s
    JOIN hero_attribute ha ON s.id = ha.hero_id
    WHERE ha.attribute_id = 2 -- Strength
    AND ha.attribute_value > 100
    UNION
    SELECT s.id, s.superhero_name, s.full_name, ha.attribute_value
    FROM superhero s
    JOIN hero_attribute ha ON s.id = ha.hero_id
    WHERE ha.attribute_id = 4 -- Durability
    AND ha.attribute_value > 80
),
SignificantPowers AS (
    SELECT ph.*
    FROM PowerfulHeroes ph
    JOIN hero_power hp ON ph.id = hp.hero_id
    GROUP BY ph.id, ph.superhero_name, ph.full_name, ph.attribute_value
    HAVING COUNT(hp.power_id) > 3
)
SELECT sp.superhero_name, sp.full_name, sp.attribute_value, a.attribute_name
FROM SignificantPowers sp
JOIN attribute a ON (sp.attribute_value > 80 AND a.id = 1) OR (sp.attribute_value > 100 AND a.id = 2) OR (sp.attribute_value > 80 AND a.id = 4)
hidden_knowledge: By significant power attributes, I mean superheroes who excel in at least one major attribute (intelligence over 90, strength over 100, or durability over 80) and have more than three distinct powers.

question: Display superheroes with significant power attributes
sql:
WITH HeroPowerStats AS (
    SELECT 
        s.id,
        s.superhero_name,
        s.full_name,
        COUNT(hp.power_id) AS power_count,
        AVG(ha.attribute_value) AS avg_attribute_score,
        MAX(ha.attribute_value) AS max_attribute_score,
        MAX(CASE WHEN ha.attribute_id = 1 THEN ha.attribute_value ELSE NULL END) AS intelligence_score,
        MAX(CASE WHEN ha.attribute_id = 2 THEN ha.attribute_value ELSE NULL END) AS strength_score,
        MAX(CASE WHEN ha.attribute_id = 4 THEN ha.attribute_value ELSE NULL END) AS durability_score
    FROM superhero s
    LEFT JOIN hero_power hp ON s.id = hp.hero_id
    LEFT JOIN hero_attribute ha ON s.id = ha.hero_id
    GROUP BY s.id, s.superhero_name, s.full_name
),
PowerfulHeroes AS (
    SELECT * FROM HeroPowerStats 
    WHERE 
        (intelligence_score IS NOT NULL AND intelligence_score > 90) OR 
        (strength_score IS NOT NULL AND strength_score > 100) OR 
        (durability_score IS NOT NULL AND durability_score > 80)
)
SELECT ph.superhero_name, ph.full_name,
       ph.power_count,
       ph.intelligence_score AS intelligence,
       ph.strength_score AS strength,
       ph.durability_score AS durability,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ph.power_count DESC) AS power_rank,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ph.intelligence_score DESC) AS intelligence_rank,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ph.strength_score DESC) AS strength_rank,
       RANK() OVER (ORDER BY ph.durability_score DESC) AS durability_rank
FROM PowerfulHeroes ph
hidden_knowledge: By significant power attributes, I mean superheroes who stand out in any of the major attributes (intelligence over 90, strength over 100, or durability over 80), and I want to see their rankings across all these dimensions along with their power count.

question: Who are the most popular superheroes based on their attributes and powers?
sql: SELECT s.superhero_name FROM superhero s JOIN hero_attribute ha ON s.id = ha.hero_id WHERE ha.attribute_value > 75 AND EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM hero_power hp WHERE hp.hero_id = s.id) GROUP BY s.superhero_name ORDER BY COUNT(ha.attribute_id) DESC LIMIT 10;
hidden_knowledge: By 'most popular', I mean superheroes with more than three high-value attributes (over 75) who also have at least one power.

question: I'm preparing a presentation on superhero diversity and need to highlight heroes with 'many' superpowers. This will help us demonstrate the range of abilities in our dataset.
sql: SELECT s.superhero_name, COUNT(hp.power_id) AS number_of_powers FROM superhero s LEFT JOIN hero_power hp ON s.id = hp.hero_id GROUP BY s.id HAVING COUNT(hp.power_id) > 5;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many' superpowers, I mean heroes with more than 5 superpowers.

question: For our upcoming documentary on superhero diversity, I need to identify superheroes with many distinct attributes and powers. This will help us showcase the complexity and variety in superhero abilities across different publishers.
sql:
WITH HeroFeatures AS (
    SELECT h.superhero_name,
           p.publisher_name,
           COUNT(DISTINCT ha.attribute_id) as attribute_count,
           COUNT(DISTINCT hp.power_id) as power_count,
           COUNT(DISTINCT ha.attribute_id) + COUNT(DISTINCT hp.power_id) as total_features,
           PERCENT_RANK() OVER (ORDER BY COUNT(DISTINCT ha.attribute_id) + COUNT(DISTINCT hp.power_id)) as feature_rank 
    FROM superhero h 
    LEFT JOIN publisher p ON h.publisher_id = p.id 
    LEFT JOIN hero_attribute ha ON h.id = ha.hero_id 
    LEFT JOIN hero_power hp ON h.id = hp.hero_id 
    GROUP BY h.superhero_name, p.publisher_name 
)
SELECT superhero_name, publisher_name,
       attribute_count, power_count, total_features 
FROM HeroFeatures 
WHERE feature_rank <= 0.2 
ORDER BY total_features DESC;
hidden_knowledge: By 'many distinct attributes and powers,' I mean that the superhero should be among the top 20% with the highest combined count of distinct attributes and powers.

question: List all customers who spent more than the average spending of their segment at any of our chain's gas stations.
sql:
WITH CustomerSpending AS (
    SELECT t.CustomerID,
           SUM(t.Amount * t.Price) AS TotalSpending,
           c.Segment
    FROM transactions_1k t
    JOIN gasstations g ON t.GasStationID = g.GasStationID
    JOIN customers c ON t.CustomerID = c.CustomerID
    WHERE g.ChainID = 6
    GROUP BY t.CustomerID
),
SegmentAverages AS (
    SELECT Segment,
           AVG(TotalSpending) AS AvgSegmentSpending
    FROM CustomerSpending
    GROUP BY Segment
)
SELECT cs.CustomerID,
       cs.TotalSpending,
       cs.Segment,
       sa.AvgSegmentSpending
FROM CustomerSpending cs
JOIN SegmentAverages sa ON cs.Segment = sa.Segment
WHERE cs.TotalSpending > sa.AvgSegmentSpending;

question: Hey there! Could you figure out my team's average player rating from last season using their most recent stats? I want an idea of how we're doing compared to others!
sql:
WITH TeamPlayersCTE AS (
    SELECT DISTINCT 
        m.home_player_1 AS player_api_id 
    FROM 
        Match m 
    WHERE 
        m.home_team_api_id = 9987 
        OR m.away_team_api_id = 9987
    UNION ALL
    SELECT DISTINCT 
        m.home_player_2 FROM Match m WHERE m.home_team_api_id = 9987 OR m.away_team_api_id = 9987
    -- Repeat UNION ALL for home_player_3 through home_player_11 and away_player_1 through away_player_11
),
LatestAttributesCTE AS (
    SELECT 
        pa.*,
        ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY pa.player_fifa_api_id ORDER BY pa.date DESC) AS rn
    FROM 
        Player_Attributes pa
    JOIN 
        TeamPlayersCTE tpc ON pa.player_fifa_api_id = tpc.player_api_id
),
FilteredAttributesCTE AS (
    SELECT 
        * 
    FROM 
        LatestAttributesCTE
    WHERE 
        rn = 1
)
SELECT 
    AVG(overall_rating) AS avg_team_rating
FROM 
    FilteredAttributesCTE;

question: Provide an analysis showing how our institution ranks among its peers within our educational option type according to FRPM percentages.
sql:
WITH PeersByEducationalOption AS (
    SELECT 
        frpm.CDSCode,
        frpm.'Educational Option Type',
        frpm.'Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12)' AS FRPMPct,
        RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY frpm.'Educational Option Type' ORDER BY frpm.'Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12)' DESC) AS FRPMRank
    FROM frpm
    JOIN schools
        ON frpm.CDSCode = schools.CDSCode
    WHERE schools.District = 'Alameda County Office of Education'
      AND frpm.'Educational Option Type' = 'Traditional'
),
MyInstitutionComparison AS (
    SELECT 
        p.'Educational Option Type',
        p.FRPMPct,
        p.FRPMRank,
        AVG(p.FRPMPct) OVER (PARTITION BY p.'Educational Option Type') AS AvgPeerFRPMPct
    FROM PeersByEducationalOption p
)
SELECT 
    'Educational Option Type',
    FRPMPct,
    FRPMRank,
    AvgPeerFRPMPct,
    ROUND((FRPMPct - AvgPeerFRPMPct), 2) AS DeviationFromAvg
FROM MyInstitutionComparison;
hidden_knowledge: The user's institution is part of the Alameda County Office of Education and falls under the 'Traditional' educational option type.

question: Provide a detailed breakdown of income sources for members in our college, categorized by member position and including the total income received per source.
sql:
WITH CollegeIncome AS (
    SELECT i.income_id, i.date_received, i.amount, i.source, i.notes,
           m.position,
           COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY i.source) AS source_count,
           SUM(i.amount) OVER (PARTITION BY i.source) AS total_source_amount,
           COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY i.source, m.position) AS position_source_count,
           SUM(i.amount) OVER (PARTITION BY i.source, m.position) AS total_position_source_amount
    FROM income i
    JOIN member m ON i.link_to_member = m.member_id
    JOIN major maj ON m.link_to_major = maj.major_id
    WHERE maj.college = 'School of Business'
)
SELECT DISTINCT source,
       position,
       COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY source) - COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY source) + source_count AS member_count_per_source,
       SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY source) - SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY source) + total_source_amount AS total_income_per_source,
       COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY source, position) - COUNT(*) OVER (PARTITION BY source, position) + position_source_count AS member_count_per_position_per_source,
       SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY source, position) - SUM(amount) OVER (PARTITION BY source, position) + total_position_source_amount AS total_income_per_position_per_source
FROM CollegeIncome;
hidden_knowledge: The user's college is School of Business.

question: Retrieve the complete race results for all Grand Prix events in which our team participated, including detailed performance metrics for each driver and constructor.
sql:
WITH TeamRaces AS (
    SELECT r.raceId, r.name, r.year, r.round
    FROM races r
    JOIN constructorResults cr ON r.raceId = cr.raceId
    JOIN constructors c ON cr.constructorId = c.constructorId
    WHERE c.constructorId = 1
),
TeamDriversResults AS (
    SELECT 
        tr.raceId,
        tr.name AS raceName,
        tr.year,
        tr.round,
        d.driverRef,
        d.forename,
        d.surname,
        rs.position,
        rs.points,
        rs.laps,
        rs.fastestLap,
        rs.fastestLapTime,
        s.status
    FROM TeamRaces tr
    JOIN results rs ON tr.raceId = rs.raceId
    JOIN drivers d ON rs.driverId = d.driverId
    JOIN status s ON rs.statusId = s.statusId
    WHERE rs.constructorId = 1
),
DriverPerformanceMetrics AS (
    SELECT 
        tdr.*,
        AVG(lt.milliseconds) OVER (PARTITION BY tdr.raceId) AS averageLapTime,
        COUNT(ps.stop) OVER (PARTITION BY tdr.raceId, tdr.driverRef) AS pitStopCount,
        SUM(ps.milliseconds) OVER (PARTITION BY tdr.raceId, tdr.driverRef) AS totalPitStopTime
    FROM TeamDriversResults tdr
    LEFT JOIN lapTimes lt ON tdr.raceId = lt.raceId AND lt.driverId = (
        SELECT driverId FROM drivers WHERE driverRef = tdr.driverRef
    )
    LEFT JOIN pitStops ps ON tdr.raceId = ps.raceId AND ps.driverId = (
        SELECT driverId FROM drivers WHERE driverRef = tdr.driverRef
    )
),
ConstructorPerformance AS (
    SELECT 
        tr.raceId,
        tr.name AS raceName,
        tr.year,
        tr.round,
        c.name AS constructorName,
        cs.position AS constructorPosition,
        cs.points AS constructorPoints,
        cs.wins AS constructorWins
    FROM TeamRaces tr
    JOIN constructorStandings cs ON tr.raceId = cs.raceId
    JOIN constructors c ON cs.constructorId = c.constructorId
)
SELECT 
    dpm.raceName AS RaceName,
    dpm.year AS Year,
    dpm.round AS RoundNumber,
    dpm.forename AS DriverFirstName,
    dpm.surname AS DriverLastName,
    dpm.position AS DriverPosition,
    dpm.points AS DriverPoints,
    dpm.laps AS LapsCompleted,
    dpm.fastestLapTime AS FastestLapTime,
    dpm.status AS RaceStatus,
    dpm.pitStopCount AS NumberOfPitStops,
    dpm.totalPitStopTime AS TotalPitStopTimeInMilliseconds,
    cp.constructorPosition AS ConstructorPosition,
    cp.constructorPoints AS ConstructorPoints
FROM DriverPerformanceMetrics dpm
JOIN ConstructorPerformance cp ON dpm.raceId = cp.raceId
ORDER BY dpm.year, dpm.round, dpm.position;
hidden_knowledge: The user is affiliated with the McLaren team, which has a constructor ID of 1 in the database.

question: Get the detailed standings for my team across all seasons
sql:
WITH detailed_team_standings AS (
  SELECT 
      s.year,
      cs.position,
      cs.points,
      cs.wins,
      cs.positionText,
      RANK() OVER (PARTITION BY s.year ORDER BY cs.points DESC) as season_rank,
      SUM(cs.points) OVER (ORDER BY s.year) as cumulative_points,
      AVG(cs.points) OVER (ORDER BY s.year ROWS BETWEEN 2 PRECEDING AND CURRENT ROW) as rolling_avg_points 
  FROM 
      seasons s 
  JOIN 
      races r ON s.year = r.year 
  JOIN 
      constructorStandings cs ON r.raceId = cs.raceId AND cs.constructorId = (SELECT constructorId FROM constructors WHERE name = 'Red Bull')
)
SELECT *
FROM detailed_team_standings
hidden_knowledge: The user's team is Red Bull.

question: What are the top 5 transaction dates with the highest sums for accounts owned by clients from my district?
sql:
WITH DistrictClients AS (
    SELECT client_id
    FROM client
    WHERE district_id = 18
),
ClientAccounts AS (
    SELECT a.account_id
    FROM account a
    JOIN disp d ON a.account_id = d.account_id
    WHERE d.client_id IN (SELECT client_id FROM DistrictClients) AND d.type = 'OWNER'
),
DateSums AS (
    SELECT 
        t.date,
        SUM(t.amount) AS total_amount,
        RANK() OVER (ORDER BY SUM(t.amount) DESC) as amount_rank 
    FROM trans t 
        JOIN ClientAccounts ca ON t.account_id = ca.account_id 
        GROUP BY t.date 
)
SELECT date, total_amount 
FROM DateSums 
WHERE amount_rank <= 5 
ORDER BY amount_rank;

question: Hey, could you show me all the molecules that contain my favorite element? I'm really curious about their structures!
sql:
WITH FavoriteElements AS (
    SELECT molecule_id
    FROM atom
    WHERE element = 'c'
    GROUP BY molecule_id
    HAVING COUNT(*) > 0
)
SELECT m.molecule_id, m.label, a.atom_id, a.element, b.bond_type
FROM molecule m
JOIN atom a ON m.molecule_id = a.molecule_id
JOIN connected c ON a.atom_id = c.atom_id OR a.atom_id = c.atom_id2
JOIN bond b ON c.bond_id = b.bond_id
WHERE m.molecule_id IN (SELECT molecule_id FROM FavoriteElements)
ORDER BY m.molecule_id, a.atom_id;

question: Retrieve the performance attributes of all players who are currently on my team's roster and have a potential rating higher than 80.
sql:
WITH CurrentTeamPlayers AS (
    SELECT p.player_api_id, p.player_name
    FROM Player p
    JOIN Match m ON p.player_api_id = m.home_player_1 OR p.player_api_id = m.home_player_2 OR
                               p.player_api_id = m.home_player_3 OR p.player_api_id = m.home_player_4 OR
                               p.player_api_id = m.home_player_5 OR p.player_api_id = m.home_player_6 OR
                               p.player_api_id = m.home_player_7 OR p.player_api_id = m.home_player_8 OR
                               p.player_api_id = m.home_player_9 OR p.player_api_id = m.home_player_10 OR
                               p.player_api_id = m.home_player_11 OR
                               p.player_api_id = m.away_player_1 OR p.player_api_id = m.away_player_2 OR
                               p.player_api_id = m.away_player_3 OR p.player_api_id = m.away_player_4 OR
                               p.player_api_id = m.away_player_5 OR p.player_api_id = m.away_player_6 OR
                               p.player_api_id = m.away_player_7 OR p.player_api_id = m.away_player_8 OR
                               p.player_api_id = m.away_player_9 OR p.player_api_id = m.away_player_10 OR
                               p.player_api_id = m.away_player_11
    WHERE m.home_team_api_id = 9987 OR m.away_team_api_id = 9987 -- Assuming the user's team is KRC Genk (team_api_id 9987)
    GROUP BY p.player_api_id, p.player_name
)
SELECT pa.*
FROM Player_Attributes pa
JOIN CurrentTeamPlayers ctp ON pa.player_api_id = ctp.player_api_id
WHERE pa.potential > 80 AND pa.date IN (
    SELECT MAX(date)
    FROM Player_Attributes
    GROUP BY player_api_id
);

question: As part of our annual review process, I need to evaluate the academic performance of students in our charter schools compared to non-charter schools within the same county. Please provide a detailed comparison of SAT scores and FRPM eligibility percentages for all schools in our county, segmented by charter status. This information is crucial for assessing the effectiveness of our charter school programs.
sql: WITH SchoolPerformance AS ( SELECT s.CDSCode, s.School, CASE WHEN s.Charter = 1 THEN 'Charter' ELSE 'Non-Charter' END AS SchoolType, AVG(sc.AvgScrRead) AS AvgReadScore, AVG(sc.AvgScrMath) AS AvgMathScore, AVG(sc.AvgScrWrite) AS AvgWriteScore, AVG(f.'Percent (%) Eligible FRPM (K-12)') AS AvgFRPM_Percent FROM schools s LEFT JOIN satscores sc ON s.CDSCode = sc.cds LEFT JOIN frpm f ON s.CDSCode = f.CDSCode WHERE s.County = 'Alameda' GROUP BY s.CDSCode, s.School, CASE WHEN s.Charter = 1 THEN 'Charter' ELSE 'Non-Charter' END ) SELECT sp.SchoolType, COUNT(sp.CDSCode) AS NumSchools, AVG(sp.AvgReadScore) AS AvgReadScore, AVG(sp.AvgMathScore) AS AvgMathScore, AVG(sp.AvgWriteScore) AS AvgWriteScore, AVG(sp.AvgFRPM_Percent) AS AvgFRPM_Percent FROM SchoolPerformance sp GROUP BY sp.SchoolType;

question: List the superheroes whose attributes most closely match my own.
sql:
WITH UserAttributes AS (
    SELECT 1 AS attribute_id, 85 AS attribute_value
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 2 AS attribute_id, 70 AS attribute_value
    UNION ALL
    SELECT 3 AS attribute_id, 60 AS attribute_value
),
SuperheroScores AS (
    SELECT 
        h.id,
        SUM(CASE WHEN ha.attribute_id = ua.attribute_id THEN ABS(ha.attribute_value - ua.attribute_value) ELSE 0 END) AS total_difference
    FROM superhero h
    JOIN hero_attribute ha ON h.id = ha.hero_id
    JOIN UserAttributes ua ON ha.attribute_id = ua.attribute_id
    GROUP BY h.id
) 
SELECT h.superhero_name
FROM SuperheroScores ss
JOIN superhero h ON ss.id = h.id
ORDER BY ss.total_difference ASC
LIMIT 5;
hidden_knowledge: The user's attributes are: Intelligence = 85, Strength = 70, Speed = 60.

hidden_knowledge: I mean the total K-12 student population from the FRPM data, not SAT-related enrollment.

question: I'm analyzing user engagement patterns on our platform and would like to understand when our most active users first demonstrated significant participation. To do this, I need to find the creation date of the earliest posts made by users who have earned the 'Teacher' badge, along with their current reputation scores. This information will help us identify early influential contributors and track their growth over time.
sql: SELECT ph.UserDisplayName, u.Reputation, MIN(ph.CreationDate) AS earliest_history_date FROM postHistory ph INNER JOIN users u ON ph.UserId = u.Id INNER JOIN badges b ON u.Id = b.UserId WHERE b.Name = 'Teacher' GROUP BY ph.UserDisplayName, u.Reputation HAVING MIN(ph.CreationDate) IS NOT NULL;
hidden_knowledge: I want the first date any revision or history entry was created for these users' posts, regardless of whether it was the original post or a later edit.

question: Retrieve the segment information for all customers who made transactions at gas stations in the 'CZE' country during the month of August 2012.
sql: SELECT g.Segment, COUNT(t.TransactionID) AS transaction_count FROM gasstations g INNER JOIN transactions_1k t ON g.GasStationID = t.GasStationID WHERE g.Country = 'CZE' AND strftime('%Y%m', t.Date) = '201208' GROUP BY g.Segment HAVING COUNT(t.TransactionID) > 0;
hidden_knowledge: I want to know the segment classification of the gas stations in the Czech Republic where transactions occurred in August 2012.

question: List all transactions with their corresponding customer segments for the premium gas stations.
sql: SELECT t.TransactionID, t.Date, t.Time, c.Segment AS Customer_Segment, g.Segment AS Gas_Station_Segment FROM transactions_1k t JOIN customers c ON t.CustomerID = c.CustomerID JOIN gasstations g ON t.GasStationID = g.GasStationID WHERE g.Segment = 'Premium';
hidden_knowledge: I want to see the segment of both the customer and the gas station for transactions at premium gas stations.

question: List all transactions with their corresponding customer segments for the premium gas stations.
sql: SELECT t.TransactionID, t.Date, t.Time, c.Segment FROM transactions_1k t JOIN customers c ON t.CustomerID = c.CustomerID WHERE EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM gasstations g WHERE g.GasStationID = t.GasStationID AND g.Segment = 'Premium');
hidden_knowledge: I only need the customer segment for transactions at premium gas stations, not the gas station segment.

question: Transaction details for EUR customers

hidden_knowledge: I want the individual transaction details for EUR customers.

question: When were the patients with SLE first diagnosed, and what were their laboratory results at that time?
sql: SELECT p.ID, p.Diagnosis, p.'First Date', l.Date, l.GOT, l.GPT FROM Patient p INNER JOIN Laboratory l ON p.ID = l.ID WHERE p.Diagnosis LIKE '%SLE%' AND p.'First Date' = l.Date;
hidden_knowledge: I mean the date when patients were first diagnosed with SLE as recorded in the Patient table, not any subsequent examination dates.

question: I'm conducting a longitudinal study on autoimmune disease progression. To analyze the temporal relationship between symptoms and test results, I need to examine all patient records where symptoms first appeared before a specific examination date. This will help me understand how early symptoms correlate with later laboratory findings and how diagnostic accuracy evolves over time.
sql: SELECT p.ID, e.'Examination Date', e.Symptoms, (SELECT Date FROM Laboratory WHERE ID = p.ID ORDER BY Date ASC LIMIT 1) AS First_Lab_Date FROM Patient p INNER JOIN Examination e ON p.ID = e.ID WHERE e.Symptoms IS NOT NULL AND (SELECT Date FROM Laboratory WHERE ID = p.ID ORDER BY Date ASC LIMIT 1) < e.'Examination Date';
hidden_knowledge: I mean the date of the patient's first laboratory test, not just any description date in their patient record. I want to compare symptom onset with actual laboratory testing timelines.

question: Patient date for SLE diagnosis.

hidden_knowledge: I mean the birth date of patients diagnosed with SLE.

question: Retrieve the elements that are part of molecules labeled with a positive charge and identify their connectivity with other atoms.
sql: SELECT a1.atom_id, a1.element, c.bond_id, a2.atom_id AS connected_atom_id, a2.element AS connected_element FROM atom a1 INNER JOIN connected c ON a1.atom_id = c.atom_id INNER JOIN molecule m ON a1.molecule_id = m.molecule_id INNER JOIN atom a2 ON c.atom_id2 = a2.atom_id INNER JOIN bond b ON c.bond_id = b.bond_id WHERE m.label = '+' AND a1.element IS NOT NULL;
hidden_knowledge: I am interested in the elements that are part of positively charged molecules and their direct connections to other atoms within the same molecule.

question: Retrieve the elements that are part of molecules labeled with a positive charge and identify their connectivity with other atoms.
sql: SELECT b.bond_id, b.bond_type, c.atom_id, a1.element AS element1, c.atom_id2, a2.element AS element2 FROM bond b INNER JOIN connected c ON b.bond_id = c.bond_id INNER JOIN molecule m ON b.molecule_id = m.molecule_id INNER JOIN atom a1 ON c.atom_id = a1.atom_id INNER JOIN atom a2 ON c.atom_id2 = a2.atom_id WHERE m.label = '+' AND (a1.element IS NOT NULL OR a2.element IS NOT NULL);
hidden_knowledge: I want to understand the types of bonds and the elements involved in the connections between atoms within positively charged molecules.

question: Hey, could you show me the connections for chlorine atoms in molecules? I'm particularly interested in seeing which bonds they're part of.
sql: SELECT a.atom_id, c.atom_id2, b.bond_type FROM atom a INNER JOIN connected c ON a.atom_id = c.atom_id INNER JOIN bond b ON c.bond_id = b.bond_id WHERE a.element = 'cl' GROUP BY a.atom_id, c.atom_id2, b.bond_type;
hidden_knowledge: I'm looking for connections where chlorine is the first atom in the connection (so chlorine to other elements).

question: Hey, could you show me the connections for chlorine atoms in molecules? I'm particularly interested in seeing which bonds they're part of.
sql: SELECT a1.atom_id, c.atom_id2, b.bond_type FROM atom a1 INNER JOIN connected c ON a1.atom_id = c.atom_id INNER JOIN atom a2 ON c.atom_id2 = a2.atom_id INNER JOIN bond b ON c.bond_id = b.bond_id WHERE a2.element = 'cl' GROUP BY a1.atom_id, c.atom_id2, b.bond_type;
hidden_knowledge: I'm looking for connections where chlorine is the second atom being connected to (so other elements to chlorine).

question: I'm conducting research on molecular structures and need to analyze the connectivity patterns between atoms in different chemical compounds. Specifically, I need to identify all the bonds that are connected to chlorine atoms in various molecules, along with the types of these bonds. This information will help me understand how chlorine atoms participate in molecular bonding across different compounds.
sql: WITH ChlorineAtoms AS ( SELECT atom_id, molecule_id FROM atom WHERE element = 'cl' ), BondedPairs AS ( SELECT c.atom_id, c.atom_id2, c.bond_id FROM connected c JOIN ChlorineAtoms ca ON c.atom_id = ca.atom_id OR c.atom_id2 = ca.atom_id ) SELECT bp.bond_id, b.bond_type, b.molecule_id, m.label FROM BondedPairs bp JOIN bond b ON bp.bond_id = b.bond_id JOIN molecule m ON b.molecule_id = m.molecule_id;
hidden_knowledge: What I really need is to find only those bonds that are directly connected to chlorine atoms in the molecular structures.
